results.possibleIdentity: E. Thomas Nash (Thomas Nash) is an experimental particle physicist who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He earned his PhD in High Energy Physics from Columbia University in 1970. During his time at Columbia, he was associated with the university's Nevis Laboratories, located in Irvington, New York, which is its primary center for high-energy and nuclear physics. His technical work focused on high-energy and particle physics, as well as the intersection of physics and computing. He worked at the DESY high-energy physics laboratory in Hamburg with Samuel C.C. Ting before joining the National Accelerator Laboratory (now Fermilab) in the early 1970s. At Fermilab, he played a leading role in several major experiments, including E-25 and E-516 (the Tagged Photon Spectrometer experiment), for which he served as spokesperson. Nash is particularly noted for his contributions to computing in physics; he served as the Head of the Advanced Computer Program (ACP) at Fermilab, where he led the development of ganged sets of 32-bit microprocessors to perform large-scale computations, an early form of parallel computing. His name appears in the archives of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization within the William S. Burroughs Papers at the New York Public Library, where a letter from E.A.T. dated March 18, 1970, mentions him, suggesting he was part of the network of scientists and engineers who collaborated with artists during that period.
